crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
1. The macro in "ThisDocument.cls" contains a subroutine named Document_Open(). This subroutine displays a message box with the text "Congratulations !! This is only sample. Not malicious so you do not worry about this. However be careful when you download the file that you don't know" when the document is opened. This message indicates that the macro is benign and serves as a warning to users about downloading unknown files.

2. The macro in "Module2.bas" contains a subroutine named AutoOpen(). This subroutine uses the FollowHyperlink method to open a hyperlink to a Google search query related to "squirrelwaffle malware". The purpose of this action seems to be educational or informative, as it directs the user to a search page about a known malware threat.

Overall, based on the analysis of the macros provided, there are no indications of malicious intent or behaviors present in the code. The macros appear to be harmless and serve either as informational messages or educational resources for users.

The provided VBA code contains two main components: a "Document_Open" subroutine within "ThisDocument" module and an "AutoOpen" subroutine within "Module2". 

Upon opening the document, the "Document_Open" subroutine displays a message box. This message reassures the user that the document is a harmless sample, not malicious, and advises caution when downloading unknown files. 

The "AutoOpen" subroutine, which executes automatically when the document is opened, instructs the application to open a hyperlink. This hyperlink points to a Google search query for "squirrelwaffle malware" using the user's default web browser in a new window.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Format: JSON Configuration

The provided JSON is a static database or threat intelligence feed detailing information on numerous known ransomware families (e.g., Locky, Cerber, Sodinokibi, LockBit, etc.). It describes the malicious nature, technical details (encryption, extensions), and command-and-control (C2) channels (including .onion links) associated with these threats. However, the JSON structure itself contains no executable fields (like scripts, commands, or execution triggers) that would allow for RCE, persistence, or data exfiltration simply by processing this file as a configuration. It is a descriptive catalog of external threats.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: unknown (pypi)
Version: N/A
Description: N/A (no METADATA file found)

The provided files are configuration definitions, test fixtures, boilerplate text, and code snippets belonging to the Wazuh security platform (version 4.14.3). Content includes configuration templates, test data inputs (JSON, XML), documentation structures, build scripts (Dockerfiles, shell scripts), and low-level source code snippets (C/C++ headers and implementation details) related to module logic (e.g., FIM, inventory, vulnerability scanning, database handling, cryptography). All content analyzed is consistent with the intended functionality and testing of a legitimate open-source security project. No evidence of malicious code injection, unauthorized behavior, or hidden payloads was found.
